sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 48, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 47 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the exact positions:
- Left square: cols 19-23
- Right square: cols 39-43
- Middle passage: YYY at cols 29-31, OO at cols 32-33
So the passage is at cols 29-33 (5 cells: 3 Y + 2 O).
The left square is at cols 19-23 and the right square is at cols 39-43. There's a gap of cols 24-28 (5 B's) between the left square and the passage, and cols 34-38 (5 B's) between the passage and the right square.
So the squares can't directly enter the passage because there are black cells (B) blocking them.
But wait, looking at rows 44-53, the passage "YYYOO" is surrounded by B's. So the squares can't move into the passage from the sides.
Let me look at the rows above the squares to see if there's a way to move the squares into the passage.
Looking at row 39: "BBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYYYYYYOOOOOOOOOOOOBBBBBB"
This shows the top of the rooms. The left room has "YYYYYYYY" (8 Y's) and the right room has "OOOOOOOOOOOOOOOO" (16 O's).
The passage "YYYOO" at rows 44-53 is below the main rooms.
Actually, I think I've been misinterpreting the shape. Let me look at the full ASCII to understand the layout better.
Looking at the ASCII:
- Rows 0-1: Top bar (B and W)
- Rows 2-8: Yellow and orange regions
- Rows 9-13: Black shape starts
- Rows 14-18: Black shape continues
- Rows 19-23: Yellow and orange regions inside black shape
- Rows 24-28: Black shape continues
- Rows 29-38: Yellow and orange regions
- Rows 39-43: Yellow and orange regions (top of rooms)
- Rows 44-53: Black walls with "YYYOO" passage
- Rows 54-58: Blue squares in rooms
- Row 59: Yellow and orange regions
- Row 63: Bottom bar (W and B)
The blue squares are in the bottom part of the black shape. The "rooms" where they move are:
- Left room: rows 39-58, cols 14-28 (approximately)
- Right room: rows 39-58, cols 34-48 (approximately)
The passage "YYYOO" at rows 44-53, cols 29-33 is between the two rooms.
But the squares can't move into the passage because there are black cells blocking them.
Wait, let me look at the passage more carefully. At row 44:
"BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB"
The passage is at cols 21-25 (YYYOO). But the left square is at cols 19-23. So there's an overlap at cols 21-23.
Hmm, but the passage is at rows 44-53, and the squares are at rows 54-58. So the squares are below the passage.
Let me look at row 53:
"BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOBBBBBSSSSSBBBBBO"
Wait, that's row 54. Let me check row 53.
Actually, I already printed rows 35-60 earlier. Let me look at row 53:
"BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB"
And row 54:
"BBBBBBBBBBSSSSSBBBBBYYYOOBBBBBSSSSSBBBBBO"
So the passage "YYYOO" is at rows 44-53, cols 21-25 (approximately). The squares are at rows 54-58.
The squares are below the passage. To move the squares into the passage, I would need to move them UP.
But when I moved UP earlier, the squares only moved up to rows 39-43 (for the left square) and rows 44-48 (for the right square). They couldn't move higher because of the black walls.
Actually, wait. Looking at the boundary of the black shape:
[44, 24], [44, 28], [58, 28], [58, 9], [39, 9], [39, 13], [38, 14], [29, 14], [28, 13], [28, 9], [19, 9], [19, 13], [18, 14], [14, 14], [14, 18], [13, 19]
This traces the left side of the black shape. The bottom of the left room is at row 58, and the top is at row 39 (approximately).
So the squares can move vertically between rows 39 and 58 (in steps of 5).
The passage is at rows 44-53. If I move the squares to rows 44-48 or 49-53, they would be at the same vertical level as the passage.
But the passage is at cols 21-25 (approximately), and the left square is at cols 19-23. To move the left square into the passage, I would need to move it RIGHT.
Let me try moving the left square RIGHT when it's at rows 44-48 or 49-53.
Actually, let me first move the squares to rows 44-48 (which is within the passage's vertical range).
Current: L=(54-58, 19-23), R=(54-58, 39-43)
Let me move UP once to get to rows 49-53, then try RIGHT.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move squares to rows 49-53, then try RIGHT to see if they can enter the passage
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
squares = [(n['boundary']) for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S']
print("After UP:", squares)

# Now try RIGHT
result = action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
squares = [(n['boundary']) for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S']
print("After RIGHT:", squares)
print("Changed:", result.get('board_changed'))

# Check row 49 to see passage position
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("Row 49:", repr(ascii_lines[49]))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
